lines.text: Welcome to Chapter 7.
Today is about a problem that looks like it
should have a clean answer, and doesn't.
We're going to write down three definitions of fairness,
each one completely reasonable, the kind of thing you'd
nod along to in a meeting, and then I'm
going to show you that on a single dataset
you cannot have all three at once.
So the work isn't picking the fair one.
The work is choosing, and then defending the choice.
Let's start concrete.
You've built a yes-or-no model.
Approve the loan or not, flag the defendant or
not, advance the candidate or not.
And it runs on a population with two groups.
You want it to be fair, so you sit
down to write what fair actually means.
Here's the trap:
you'll naturally write three requirements, and every one of
them sounds exactly like fairness.
The bad news, which I'm going to prove to
you, is that when the two groups have different
base rates, and in the real world they almost
always do, those three requirements fight each other.
Now, this isn't a hand-wavy fairness-is-hard sentiment.
It's a theorem, proved independently in 2016 by two
different groups, Kleinberg, Mullainathan and Raghavan on one side,
and Chouldechova on the other.
It says these three criteria are mathematically incompatible whenever
the base rates differ.
It's not a limitation of your tooling, it's not
dirty data you can scrub clean, it's structural.
And notice what a theorem like this does to
you.
It doesn't let you dodge the question.
It forces you to choose.
And choices, in engineering, have to be defended.
So let's meet the three.
This is part one.
I want you to sit with each of these
and notice two things at once:
each one captures something fairness genuinely seems to require,
and yet each is a different statement about your
model.
First one:
demographic parity, sometimes called statistical parity.
The thing to hold onto is that this is
a statement purely about the model's outputs.
It never looks at whether the model was actually
right.
It just says the model should say yes to
the same fraction of group A as group B.
Equal positive rates.
Whatever the prediction is, hand it out evenly.
Picture a bank.
It approves loans in one neighborhood at forty percent,
and in another at eighteen percent.
Demographic parity looks at that and says the loan
itself, the thing that buys a house or starts
a business, should reach both groups at the same
rate, full stop.
It deliberately does not ask whether default rates differ
between the groups.
The claim is about the outcome people actually live
with, not about the model's accuracy.
Second:
equalized odds.
This one does look at the truth.
It says, among the people who really are positive,
the model should catch them at the same rate
in both groups, that's equal true-positive rates.
And among the people who really are negative, it
should falsely flag them at the same rate, that's
equal false-positive rates.
So where demographic parity was about outputs, equalized odds
is about errors, and specifically about spreading your errors
evenly across the groups.
Here's why that matters.
Take a hiring screen that rejects qualified candidates twice
as often in one group as the other.
Two people, equally capable, different groups, and one of
them is far more likely to be wrongly turned
away.
That group is quietly absorbing more of the harm
the model does when it's wrong.
Equalized odds is the criterion that says the cost
of your mistakes has to fall evenly.
Third:
calibration, or predictive parity.
This one is about whether the model's probabilities are
honest.
It says that when the model outputs a number,
say seventy percent, that number has to mean an
actual seventy percent chance, and it has to mean
the same thing for everyone, regardless of group.
A score is a promise about reality, and calibration
is the demand that the promise hold equally for
both groups.
Think about a doctor reading a hospital's readmission risk
score.
It says high risk, zero point eight, and the
doctor acts on it.
And crucially, the doctor doesn't know, and shouldn't need
to know, which group the patient belongs to.
For that to be safe, zero point eight has
to mean zero point eight for everybody.
The moment the same score means different things for
different groups, you've quietly handed the clinician a biased
instrument.
So line them up.
Demographic parity cares about the visible outputs.
Equalized odds cares about who bears the errors.
Calibration cares about whether the scores tell the truth.
Three different statements, but really, three different values.
And I want to be clear:
none of these is the obviously correct one.
Each is a coherent moral position, and that's exactly
what makes the next part uncomfortable.
Part two.
I don't want you to take the impossibility on
my authority, or even on the authority of a
theorem you haven't watched get worked out.
So we're going to make it obvious with numbers.
Once you see the arithmetic, you can't un-see it.
Here's the whole setup, and it really is this
simple.
Group A has a base rate of sixty percent
truly positive.
Group B, thirty percent.
One number, the base rate, does all of the
damage.
And we'll assume the classifier is calibrated, meaning that
among everyone it scores at probability p, the real
positive rate is p, honestly, in both groups.
Now just watch what that one assumption forces.
The quantity to track is positive predictive value, which
is just precision.
Of the people you flagged positive, how many actually
were positive?
And here's the key fact:
precision depends on the base rate.
Where positives are common, a flag is more likely
to be a real one.
Where positives are rare, that exact same flag is
more likely to be a false alarm, even with
an identical model.
The cleanest intuition I know for this is a
smoke alarm.
One detector, one sensitivity, fixed.
Put it in the kitchen, where fires are relatively
common, and most of its alarms are real.
Take the identical alarm, put it in the bedroom,
where fires are rare, and most of its alarms
are false.
Same device.
The trustworthiness of the alarm changed because the base
rate changed.
Your classifier is that alarm, and your two groups
are the two rooms.
You can write this down exactly, and Chouldechova did.
It falls straight out of Bayes' rule:
there's a fixed relationship between precision, the base rate,
and your error rates.
And once you stare at that relationship, the conclusion
is forced.
If two groups have different base rates, you cannot
keep precision equal and keep your error rates equal
at the same time.
It's not a modeling choice.
It's algebra.
There's no threshold, no clever loss function, that gets
you out of it.
Here's the theorem as a picture.
Three corners, the three criteria, and you can satisfy
any two of them.
Pick any pair you like, and the third one
breaks.
Watch it cycle.
Keep calibration and equalized odds, parity goes.
Keep parity and calibration, the odds go.
There is no setting that holds all three.
You are always choosing which one to give up.
And this is the same model, just two different
threshold choices, drawn as bars.
Move the threshold to satisfy calibration, and the error
rates split apart between the groups.
Move it to equalize the odds, and the calibration
breaks.
You're not fixing anything by sliding that threshold.
You're only choosing what breaks.
The trade-off is a steering wheel, not an escape
hatch.
Part three.
Let's leave the abstract and go to the most
famous time this theorem detonated in public.
Broward County, Florida, 2016.
A courtroom risk score, and a fight that, underneath
all the headlines, was this exact theorem playing out,
with real people's freedom on the line.
The tool was called COMPAS, and it fed into
bail and sentencing decisions.
ProPublica investigated and found that Black defendants who were
not re-arrested had been flagged high-risk about twice as
often as white defendants who weren't.
That's a false-positive-rate gap, a violation of equalized odds.
Northpointe, the company, fired back:
within any given risk score, the re-offend rate was
about the same across groups, so the tool was
calibrated.
And here's the thing you have to absorb.
They were both right.
Both pictures were true at the same time.
ProPublica's chart showing the error gap, and Northpointe's chart
showing nearly identical re-offend rates within each score.
Neither side was lying, neither side was fudging numbers.
The disagreement was never about the data.
It was about which definition of fairness the tool
owed people.
That's the theorem, wearing a courtroom's clothes.
So here's the lesson of the chapter, and if
you take one thing away, take this.
Each of these metrics is a coherent value, and
none of them is the automatically-right one.
Which one you owe depends on the deployment:
who's using the prediction, what decision they make with
it, who pays for the model's mistakes, and whether
the base-rate difference itself is something you think is
just or unjust.
The math is settled.
The choice is yours, and it is a moral
one.
Part four.
Everything so far has been about groups, and group
fairness has a real blind spot.
A model can satisfy demographic parity perfectly and still
treat two nearly-identical people completely differently, as long as
the group totals come out even.
So let's bring in the older intuition, the Aristotelian
one:
similar individuals should be treated similarly.
Dwork and colleagues formalized this in 2012 with what's
called a Lipschitz condition.
In plain words:
the difference in how the model treats two people
can be no larger than how different those two
people actually are.
Close people get close treatment.
The model isn't allowed to suddenly swing between two
applicants who are basically the same.
It's a smoothness requirement, placed on fairness itself.
Two loan applicants, identical finances, different ZIP codes.
The whole guarantee now rests on one thing:
your similarity metric, the function that decides how different
two people really are.
If that metric ignores ZIP, the model must treat
them alike.
But if it encodes ZIP as a genuine difference,
then different treatment becomes permitted, and you've just smuggled
structural inequity into the very condition that was supposed
to protect against it.
The guarantee is only as honest as the distance
function underneath it.
Now the tempting shortcut:
just delete the sensitive attribute.
Drop race, drop gender, and surely the model can't
discriminate.
This is called fairness through unawareness, and it is
intuitive and mathematically weak.
The reason is proxies.
ZIP code, surname, occupation quietly reconstruct the attribute you
deleted.
Omit race but keep ZIP, and the model can
still find race.
Deleting the column is a documentation choice, not a
guarantee.
Part five.
Up to now, every metric just reads the data
as it sits.
But some fairness questions can't be answered from the
data alone, because you have to distinguish discrimination from
a disparity that's actually justified, and those two can
produce data that looks identical.
To tell them apart, you need a model of
how the data was generated in the first place.
A correlation between group and prediction can come about
three different ways.
It can be direct, where the group affects the
decision straight up.
It can be indirect, running through some legitimate mediator.
Or it can be spurious, driven by a confounder
sitting behind both.
Disparate treatment is the direct path;
disparate impact is the indirect one, and the right
response is completely different for each.
The uncomfortable part:
your observational metrics can't tell these three apart.
They see one correlation and conflate all three causes.
Kusner and colleagues, in 2017, gave us a sharp
version of this, called counterfactual fairness.
The question it asks is beautifully direct:
would this exact individual have gotten the same decision
if they had belonged to a different group, holding
everything else about them fixed?
That's an intervention, in the do-calculus sense, run at
the level of a single person.
If flipping their group would change the decision, then
the model isn't counterfactually fair to them.
Here's how a model that only looks at education
can still encode race.
Historical access to education was itself shaped by race,
so race flows into the credential, and the credential
predicts default.
Now run the counterfactual:
change this applicant's race, hold everything else fixed, and
their credential would change too, because of that historical
pathway, so the prediction changes.
Counterfactual fairness, violated, even though race was never an
input.
The remedy is to exclude variables that sit downstream
of group along an illegitimate path.
Part six.
Every metric so far hands you a verdict:
fair or not fair, a gap or no gap.
But sometimes you want a dial, not a verdict.
How much unfairness is there, and where is it
coming from?
For that, we borrow a tool from economics, the
same family of indices used to measure income inequality.
It's called the Generalized Entropy Index.
The idea:
give each individual a benefit, essentially how well the
model treated them relative to what they deserved, and
then measure how unequally that benefit is distributed across
everyone, exactly the way economists measure how unequally income
is spread.
There's a parameter, alpha, that recovers specific famous indices:
mean log deviation at zero, the Theil index at
one, and at two something close to the squared
coefficient of variation, which is a common default.
That alpha isn't a technicality, it's a values knob.
A low alpha makes the index scream about the
worst-served people, the bottom of the distribution.
A high alpha makes it more responsive to the
top.
Same distribution of benefit, different alpha, different number, because
you are choosing whose harm you most want the
metric to notice.
Picking alpha is itself a moral decision about who
you're trying to protect.
And here's its best trick.
The total unfairness splits cleanly into two pieces:
the part that lives between groups, and the part
that lives within them.
A standard group audit only ever sees the between-group
term.
It's completely blind to inequity happening inside a group.
And the ratio of those two pieces actually points
you toward the remedy:
whether your problem is across groups, or hiding inside
one.
Part seven, and this is where the whole chapter
comes to rest.
Everything I've handed you so far is machinery, and
machinery is seductive, because it feels like an answer.
But notice what it actually does and doesn't do
for you.
It can carry out a decision you've already made.
It cannot make that decision, and it won't stand
up and justify it when someone asks you why.
That justification, written down and owned by you, is
the real thing you ship.
Not the metric.
The argument for the metric.
Quick tour of what you can actually reach for.
Three families.
Pre-processing:
reweight or resample the training data before you fit,
model-agnostic and upstream, but it can't fix truth that
was mis-recorded.
In-processing:
bake fairness into the objective as a constraint while
training.
Post-processing:
adjust the outputs or thresholds after the fact.
All useful, but notice none of them resolves the
impossibility theorem.
They implement a choice within it.
And none of them reaches the upstream, structural bias
that produced the data in the first place.
So here's the form your work should take, every
single time.
Six boxes.
Specify the metric you're choosing.
Compute it.
Name the conflict, say out loud which criterion you're
giving up.
State your choice.
Defend it in writing, tied to the deployment.
And, the box people love to skip, name what
would change your mind.
If you can fill these six honestly, you've done
fairness engineering.
If you can't, you've just computed a number.
Let me be honest about the ceiling.
Group metrics can't certify that two similar individuals were
treated consistently.
Individual fairness needs a similarity metric that someone has
to load with values.
Causal fairness needs a structural model the data simply
can't give you.
Each approach needs something the others can't supply, and
not one of them can tell you whether the
prediction task itself was even fair to pose in
the first place.
The tools have a hard edge, and you should
know exactly where it is.
So the supervisory move, the thing I want you
walking out with:
understand the math, choose the values, and defend the
integration.
Three formally distinct metrics can be flatly incompatible on
a single dataset the moment base rates differ.
That's structural, and it isn't going away.
What's yours to own is connecting the metric you
chose to the deployment, and to the people who
bear the cost of being wrong.
Next time, we turn from fairness to a different
kind of pressure on your model:
adversarial robustness.
